Abstract
--------

Recent advancements in protein/enzyme engineering have enabled the production of a diverse array of high-value compounds in microbial systems with the potential for industrial applications. The goal of this review is to articulate some of the most recent protein engineering advances in bacteria, yeast, and other microbial systems to produce valuable substances. These high-value substances include α-farnesene, vitamin B12, fumaric acid, linalool, glucaric acid, carminic acid, mycosporine-like amino acids, patchoulol, orcinol glucoside, d-lactic acid, keratinase, α-glucanotransferases, β-glucosidase, seleno-methylselenocysteine, fatty acids, high-efficiency β-glucosidase enzymes, cellulase, β-carotene, physcion, and glucoamylase. Additionally, recent advances in enzyme engineering for enhancing thermostability will be discussed. These findings have the potential to revolutionize various industries, including biotechnology, food, pharmaceuticals, and biofuels.

**Keywords:** protein and enzyme engineering, industrial biotechnology, thermostability, _Escherichia coli_, _Saccharomyces cerevisiae_, yeast, bacteria, fungi, algae

1. Introduction
---------------

Gene manipulation involves the use of biotechnological techniques to alter the genetic material of an organism, enabling the production of proteins with desired characteristics [[1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11193397/#j_biol-2022-0856_ref_001)]. Protein engineering has evolved over many decades ([Table 1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11193397/#j_biol-2022-0856_tab_001)), and gene manipulation is no longer a technical challenge. For instance, human genes can be successfully integrated into the genome of mice, and plant genes can even be introduced into bacterial and yeast cells. One of the notable breakthroughs was the work of Stanley Cohen and Herbert Boyer, who transferred a human gene encoding insulin into bacterial cells to produce insulin [[2](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11193397/#j_biol-2022-0856_ref_002)]. This achievement highlights the universal nature of life’s basic instructions, enabling the decoding of genetic information from various organisms to produce proteins. In the area of agriculture, protein engineering facilitates the creation of genetically modified plants (GMOs) with unique properties. Typically, their fruits are characterized by increased durability, enhanced taste, stronger aroma, resistance to pests, and adverse environmental conditions. A notable example is Flavr Savr, a breed of genetically modified tomatoes resistant to rot due to a transformed gene responsible for the production of an enzyme that breaks down the cell wall [[3](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11193397/#j_biol-2022-0856_ref_003)]. Flavr Savr has been approved and is available for human consumption. Genetic manipulation has also been used to create modified animals with increased growth hormone levels, which quickly grow to large sizes, thereby reducing breeding costs in the long run [[4](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11193397/#j_biol-2022-0856_ref_004)].

[Open in a new tab](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11193397/table/j_biol-2022-0856_tab_001/)

Designing new proteins with desired functions is a complex task, but it can yield significant benefits to the pharmaceutical, biomedical, and other industries. While medical applications currently represent the most lucrative market for engineered protein products, synthetic enzymes are also utilized in the food industry for processing. Moreover, engineered enzymes have been employed in industries to detoxify pollutants such as plastics [[5](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11193397/#j_biol-2022-0856_ref_005)]. The vast sequence space and numerous structural constraints are the major factors that make designing improved proteins difficult. Factually, a protein composed of 100 amino acids can indeed have about 10 130 possible variants, the vast majority of which are non-functional [[6](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11193397/#j_biol-2022-0856_ref_006)]. High-throughput biochemical assays and sequencing technologies have accelerated large-scale genetic screening, which is crucial for protein engineering. This has allowed for an in-depth exploration of the relationships between protein structure and function under conditions of controlled selective pressure. Such pressures could be external factors like temperature, pH, or substrate concentration, which are critical in the design and optimization of proteins for specific applications. This approach has significantly enhanced the understanding of protein evolution, adaptation, and other biological phenomena, paving the way for the development of novel proteins with desired properties.

The fundamental principle of protein engineering involves modifying sequences through methods, such as direct evolution, rational design, and _de novo_ design ([Figure 1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11193397/#j_biol-2022-0856_fig_001)). These modifications aim to enhance the catalytic activity, stability, and selectivity, thereby improving the enzyme cascade system. Significant progress has been made in protein (enzyme) engineering methods, with current techniques showing notable improvements over traditional methods ([Table 2](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11193397/#j_biol-2022-0856_tab_002)). Emerging frontiers in enzyme immobilization, computational enzyme design, multi-enzyme systems, synthetic biology, and genome editing hold promise for further advancements in enzyme engineering. With continued research and innovation, enzyme engineering has the potential to revolutionize a wide range of industries, leading to a greener and more sustainable future.

### Table 2.

Comparison between the current protein engineering methods and traditional methods

|  | Traditional methods | New methods |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Overview | Traditional protein engineering involves rational protein design and directed evolution. It often requires detailed knowledge of the structure and function of a protein | New advances leverage computational protein design to create new proteins and functions _in silico_ [[24](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11193397/#j_biol-2022-0856_ref_024)]. They also use machine learning and artificial intelligence [[25](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11193397/#j_biol-2022-0856_ref_025),[26](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11193397/#j_biol-2022-0856_ref_026)] |
| Advantages | Traditional methods are inexpensive and technically easy, as site-directed mutagenesis methods are well-developed | New methods accelerate the process, reduce costs, and enable more sophisticated engineering goals. They can also produce protein chains up to 164 amino acids long rapidly (Hartrampf et al., [[27](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11193397/#j_biol-2022-0856_ref_027)]) |
| Limitations | The major drawback is that detailed structural knowledge of a protein is often unavailable. Even when available, it is very difficult to predict the effects of various mutations | While the sequence-conformation space that needs to be searched is large, the most challenging requirement for computational protein design is a fast yet accurate energy function |
| Applications | Traditional methods have been used to improve the function of many enzymes for industrial catalysis | New methods have been applied in the design of novel protein variants incorporating amino acids that do not occur naturally in cells [[28](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11193397/#j_biol-2022-0856_ref_028)]. They have also been used in the development of biocatalysts for industrial applications |

[Open in a new tab](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11193397/table/j_biol-2022-0856_tab_002/)

To promote the industrial use of enzymes, several crucial factors must be considered, including catalytic efficiency, substrate specificity, and stability [[26](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11193397/#j_biol-2022-0856_ref_026),[29](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11193397/#j_biol-2022-0856_ref_029)]. Catalytic efficiency is a fundamental component of enzyme engineering, where it decisively influences the rate and efficacy of enzymatic reactions [[30](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11193397/#j_biol-2022-0856_ref_030),[31](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11193397/#j_biol-2022-0856_ref_031)]. In the field of industrial biotechnology, the need for highly efficient enzymes presents new challenges, particularly the necessity to significantly increase catalytic turnover (_k_ cat) or efficiency (_k_ cat/_K_ m). Substrate specificity, on the other hand, is a crucial element in enzyme engineering. It refers to an enzyme’s capacity to selectively bind and catalyze a particular substrate over others. This specificity is dictated by the unique three-dimensional structure of the enzyme’s active site, which is specifically designed to accommodate certain substrates [[32](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11193397/#j_biol-2022-0856_ref_032)]. In an industrial context, this specificity ensures that engineered enzymes execute their intended reactions with efficiency and selectivity, thereby minimizing undesired side reactions. For example, in the pharmaceutical industry, enzymes exhibiting high substrate specificity are employed to synthesize drugs, resulting in fewer impurities. Similarly, stability against heat and organic solvents is particularly important due to the harsh conditions of industrial processes. Directed evolution has been instrumental in enhancing catalytic properties, and efforts have been made to create enzymes with new catalytic functions for non-natural reactions. Rational design, computational methods, and directed evolution have been combined to achieve this goal. Likewise, substrate channeling, an approach that directs intermediates to the next-stage enzymes, has been explored to enhance reaction rates and conversion yields in multi-enzyme processes [[33](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11193397/#j_biol-2022-0856_ref_033)]. Various strategies, including co-localization of enzymes and the use of scaffold molecules, have also been employed to facilitate substrate channeling and increase product yield.

In recent years, protein/enzyme engineering has seen numerous advancements, resulting in remarkable outcomes with potential for industrial application. This review aims to spotlight these recent achievements. It specifically explores the application of bacteria, yeasts, fungi, and algae systems in enzyme engineering. Additionally, it highlights recent bioengineering efforts to enhance thermostability, a critical factor for production efficiency [[34](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11193397/#j_biol-2022-0856_ref_034)]. Overall, this review intends to enrich the awareness of scientists, industries, and stakeholders about current developments in this very important field.

### 1.1. Metabolic engineering in _Escherichia coli_ for the biosynthesis of specific compounds

_E. coli_ is a versatile and well-studied bacterium that has been extensively employed in biotechnology for the production of various industrially important compounds. Recent advancements in metabolic engineering have further highlighted the versatility of _E. coli_ in diverse bio-based chemical productions, leading to promising results in enhancing bio-products such as γ-aminobutyric acid (GABA), shikimate, d-allulose, monophosphoryl lipid A (MPL), caffeic acid, α-aminoadipic acid (AAA), lipoic acid (LA), l-arginine, erythritol, 2-phenylethanol, hydroxytyrosol (HT), 2′-fucosyllactose (2′-FL), _cis_-3-hydroxypipecolic acid (_cis_-3-HyPip), and various valuable compounds routed from heme biosynthesis using whole-cell biocatalysis. Yang et al. recently demonstrated the remarkable potential of _E. coli_ in enhancing GABA production through a combination of enzyme evolution and metabolic engineering techniques [[35](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11193397/#j_biol-2022-0856_ref_035)]. By developing mutants of glutamate decarboxylase (GadBM4), a 20.27% increase in GABA productivity in one of the mutants (GadBM4-2) was achieved. Further improvement in GABA production was achieved by introducing the regulator GadE and enzymes from the pathway for the synthesis of pyridoxal 5′-phosphate. This resulted in a remarkable 24.92% increase and reached 76.70 g/L/h with high conversion efficiency. In a 5-L capacity bioreactor, the concentration of GABA in the fermentation broth reached an impressive 307.5 ± 5.94 g/L, with a production rate of 61.49 g/L/h when crude l-glutamic acid was used as the substrate. The versatility of _E. coli_ extends to the production of shikimate, as demonstrated by Li et al. [[36](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11193397/#j_biol-2022-0856_ref_036)], where, using a comprehensive approach, they identified and utilized ten key target genes to promote metabolic flux towards shikimate biosynthesis [[36](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11193397/#j_biol-2022-0856_ref_036)]. The engineered _E. coli_ strain, SA05, achieved a remarkable shikimate production of 78.4 g/L. Further optimization reduced unwanted by-products and enhanced shikimate productivity by 23.2%. In a 30-L fermenter, SA09 achieved a notable shikimate titer of 126.4 g/L, a glucose yield of 0.50 g/g, and 2.63 g/L/h productivity [[36](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11193397/#j_biol-2022-0856_ref_036)]. This finding establishes a new benchmark for shikimate production in _E. coli_, recording the highest titer and productivity to date. Likewise, Zhang et al. devised an efficient strategy for producing d-allulose using a catalyst developed by assembling the d-allulose synthetic cascade in the _E. coli_ envelope, eliminating the need for costly purified enzymes and by-products. Process optimization strikingly improved the d-allulose titer by 1,500%, and the production was scaled up to a 3-L scale, yielding 5.67 g L−1 d-allulose [[37](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11193397/#j_biol-2022-0856_ref_037)]. This approach holds promise for sustainable d-allulose production. _E. coli_ is also amenable to genetic modification to produce MPL, a key vaccine adjuvant [[38](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11193397/#j_biol-2022-0856_ref_038)]. By removing specific gene clusters and overexpressing selected genes, a new strain, MW012, was created and was further modified into MW012/pWEPL, which produced two MPL species, with the more effective hexa-acylated MPL constituting 75% [[38](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11193397/#j_biol-2022-0856_ref_038)]. This innovative method offers a potentially more productive and cost-efficient MPL production route, with significant implications for vaccine development and wider biotechnological applications.

The phenolic compound 3,4-dihydroxycinnamic acid, commonly known as caffeic acid, is a valuable precursor for a vast array of several high-value compounds. Caffeic acid was efficiently produced in an _E. coli_ strain, CA3, engineered to express tyrosine ammonia-lyase, _p_-coumaric acid 3-hydroxylase, and feedback-resistant chorismate mutase/prephenate dehydrogenase. Using this strategy, CA3, directly converted glucose into caffeic acid, yielding 1.58 g/L without the need for tyrosine supplementation. To further enhance the yield, 4-hydroxyphenylacetate 3-hydroxylase was introduced, resulting in an engineered strain, CA8, that produced 6.17 g/L of caffeic acid directly from glucose [[39](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11193397/#j_biol-2022-0856_ref_039)]. The developed _E. coli_ strain holds promise as a framework strain for the biosynthesis of caffeic acid derivatives. In another study, both nonproteinogenic and proteinogenic amino acids have been successfully produced in engineered _E. coli_ strains to efficiently produce AAA by modifying lysine biosynthesis and degradation pathways [[40](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11193397/#j_biol-2022-0856_ref_040)]. Notably, transgenic enzymes from other organisms, including _Saccharomyces cerevisiae_ and _Rhodococcus erythropolis_, were introduced, which enhanced lysine supply while improving AAA efflux using a glutamate transporter from _Corynebacterium glutamicum_. The engineered strain achieved high AAA production levels of 2.94 g/L in flasks subjected to shaking and 5.64 g/L in bioreactors [[40](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11193397/#j_biol-2022-0856_ref_040)]. This method offers a sustainable and cost-effective alternative to chemical synthesis for industries such as pharmaceuticals, chemicals, and animal feed. Just as caffeic acid is very important industrially, l-arginine’s versatile properties, from enhancing nitric oxide production to promoting protein synthesis, make it an indispensable ingredient in various industrial applications, ranging from pharmaceutical manufacturing to food production. To improve l-arginine production, Nie et al. employed a key strategy of directly feeding _N_-acetylglutamate to engineered _E. coli_, bypassing the regulation and metabolic complexity of l-arginine biosynthesis [[41](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11193397/#j_biol-2022-0856_ref_041)]. Gene disruption (_argA_, _astA_, _speF_, _speB_, and _argR_) and operon overexpression (_argDGI_ and _argCBH_) enabled high-yield l-arginine production (4 g/L) with a yield of 0.99 mol l-arginine

Abstract
--------

Protein engineering mechanisms can be an efficient approach to enhance the biochemical properties of various [biocatalysts](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/biocatalyst "Learn more about biocatalysts from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). Immobilization of [biocatalysts](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/biocatalyst "Learn more about biocatalysts from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and the introduction of new-to-nature [chemical reactivities](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/chemical-reactivity "Learn more about chemical reactivities from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") are also possible through the same mechanism. Discovering new protocols that enhance the catalytic active protein that possesses novelty in terms of being stable, active, and, [stereoselectivity](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/immunology-and-microbiology/stereoselectivity "Learn more about stereoselectivity from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") with functions could be identified as essential areas in terms of concurrent [bioorganic chemistry](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/bioorganic-chemistry "Learn more about bioorganic chemistry from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (synergistic relationship between [organic chemistry](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/organic-chemistry "Learn more about organic chemistry from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and [biochemistry](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/biochemistry "Learn more about biochemistry from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") in the context of enzyme engineering). However, with our current level of knowledge about [protein folding](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/immunology-and-microbiology/protein-folding "Learn more about protein folding from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and its correlation with protein conformation and activities, it is almost impossible to [design proteins](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/protein-design "Learn more about design proteins from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") with specific biological and physical properties. Hence, contemporary protein engineering typically involves reprogramming existing [enzymes](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/enzyme "Learn more about enzymes from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") by [mutagenesis](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/mutagenesis "Learn more about mutagenesis from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") to generate new phenotypes with desired properties. These processes ensure that limitations of naturally occurring enzymes are not encountered. For example, researchers have engineered [cellulases](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/cellulase "Learn more about cellulases from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and hemicellulases to withstand harsh conditions encountered during biomass [pretreatment](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/pretreatment "Learn more about pretreatment from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), such as high temperatures and acidic environments. By enhancing the activity and robustness of these enzymes, biofuel production becomes more economically viable and environmentally sustainable. Recent trends in [enzyme engineering](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/immunology-and-microbiology/enzyme-engineering "Learn more about enzyme engineering from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") have enabled the development of tailored [biocatalysts](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/biocatalyst "Learn more about biocatalysts from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") for [pharmaceutical applications](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/pharmaceutical-application "Learn more about pharmaceutical applications from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). For instance, researchers have engineered enzymes such as [cytochrome](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/cytochrome "Learn more about cytochrome from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") P450s and [amine oxidases](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/amine-oxidase-flavin-containing "Learn more about amine oxidases from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") to catalyze challenging reactions involved in drug synthesis. In addition to conventional methods, there has been an increasing application of machine learning techniques to identify patterns in data. These patterns are then used to predict protein structures, enhance enzyme solubility, stability, and function, forecast [substrate specificity](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/substrate-specificity "Learn more about substrate specificity from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and assist in rational protein design. In this review, we discussed recent trends in [enzyme engineering](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/enzyme-engineering "Learn more about enzyme engineering from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") to optimize the biochemical properties of various [biocatalysts](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/immunology-and-microbiology/biocatalyst "Learn more about biocatalysts from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). Using examples relevant to biotechnology in engineering enzymes, we try to expatiate the significance of enzyme engineering with how these methods could be applied to optimize the biochemical properties of a naturally occurring enzyme.

1. Introduction
---------------

[Enzymes](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/enzyme "Learn more about Enzymes from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") are proteinous biocatalyzers that regulate intricate metabolic pathways and other cellular functions in addition to determining the rate of biological reactions [[[1]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024087048#bib1), [[2]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024087048#bib2), [[3]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024087048#bib3), [[4]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024087048#bib4)]. Enzymes as [biocatalysts](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/biocatalyst "Learn more about biocatalysts from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") are known to exhibit high efficiency and versatility. Reactions proceed far more quickly when enzymes are present than when they are not [[5](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024087048#bib5)]. These biological molecules can identify the chemicals that require transformation with extreme specificity. Over the course of development, enzymes have evolved on their own, giving rise to innovative [biocatalysts](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/biocatalyst "Learn more about biocatalysts from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") with superior qualities and a wide range of biological applications [[[5]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024087048#bib5), [[6]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024087048#bib6), [[7]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024087048#bib7), [[8]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024087048#bib8)]. All forms of life are now capable of undergoing a vast range of alterations because of their regulated evolution over time. Throughout the course of evolutionary history, all forms of life have undergone continuous adaptation and modification, leading to the development of diverse biological traits and capabilities. This evolutionary process, driven by [natural selection](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/natural-selection "Learn more about natural selection from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and [genetic](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/genetics "Learn more about genetic from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") variation, has enabled organisms to acquire a wide array of characteristics that enhance their survival and [reproductive success](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/immunology-and-microbiology/reproductive-success "Learn more about reproductive success from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") in changing environments. As a result of this regulated evolution over time, organisms possess inherent flexibility and adaptability, allowing them to undergo a vast range of alterations in response to environmental pressures, [genetic mutations](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/gene-mutation "Learn more about genetic mutations from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and other factors. This capacity for adaptation is a fundamental feature of life on Earth, shaping the diversity and resilience of living organisms across different ecosystems and evolutionary time scales. This emphasizes the dynamic nature of evolution and the role it plays in shaping the biological diversity and adaptability observed in all forms of life. As green [biocatalysts](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/immunology-and-microbiology/biocatalyst "Learn more about biocatalysts from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), they are widely used in a wide range of industries, including baking, beverages, food, textiles, leather, feed, pharmaceuticals, and detergents [[9](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024087048#bib9),[10](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024087048#bib10)]. This is because they can reduce the use of [natural resources](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/natural-resource "Learn more about natural resources from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), minimize [adverse effects](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/adverse-event "Learn more about adverse effects from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") on the environment, increase efficiency, be economically viable, and produce high-quality products [[[5]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024087048#bib5), [[6]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024087048#bib6), [[7]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024087048#bib7), [[8]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024087048#bib8),[[11]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024087048#bib11), [[12]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024087048#bib12), [[13]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024087048#bib13)]. Furthermore, [microbial enzymes](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/microbial-enzyme "Learn more about microbial enzymes from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") have the ability to either convert or degrade harmful chemical substances found in household and industrial waste, including [nitriles](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/nitrile "Learn more about nitriles from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), amines, phenolics, and [xenobiotics](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/xenobiotic-agent "Learn more about xenobiotics from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") [[2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024087048#bib2),[7](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024087048#bib7),[14](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024087048#bib14)]. Microbial and plant-based enzymes like [hydrolases](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/hydrolase "Learn more about hydrolases from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and pectinases have been utilized extensively in manufacturing food items like juice, milk and its derivatives, beer, and bread [[15](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024087048#bib15)]. Highly selective, effective, and stable enzymes have been developed for widespread application in various industries, including detergent, textiles, pharmaceutical synthesis, waste treatment in bioremediation, pulp and paper. Enzymes isolated from natural sources or in their native forms may fall short of what customers in the market need.

The main challenge with enzyme-based catalysis is the lack of effective [biocatalysts](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/biocatalyst "Learn more about biocatalysts from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") for numerous essential chemical processes with commercial relevance [[16](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024087048#bib16),[17](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024087048#bib17)]. Natural biocatalysts are limited in industrial processes by the possibility that it will be extremely inefficient when catalyzing reactions with non-cognate substrates and vulnerable to the severe conditions of chemical reactions in laboratories. Therefore, enhancing the biocatalytic efficacy of native enzymes under chemical reaction settings by higher orders of [strength](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/mechanical-strength "Learn more about strength from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") represents the ultimate challenge in biocatalysis-assisted chemical reactions. This problem is solved via enzymatic engineering [[[17]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024087048#bib17), [[18]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024087048#bib18), [[19]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024087048#bib19)]. [Enzyme engineering](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/enzyme-engineering "Learn more about Enzyme engineering from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") is now frequently used in some situations to increase the activity of enzymes and satisfy the demands of intricate industrial applications. As a result, great efforts are being made to modify enzymes in order to meet industry demands. Biocatalysts' resistance to high temperatures, severe pH values, and high levels of [organic solvents](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/organic-solvents "Learn more about organic solvents from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") has been increased by the application of enzyme engineering [[16](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024087048#bib16),[18](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024087048#bib18),[20](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024087048#bib20)]. [Enzyme engineering](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/immunology-and-microbiology/enzyme-engineering "Learn more about Enzyme engineering from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") techniques are also frequently used to engineer other features of enzymes, such as specific activity, [substrate specificity](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/substrate-specificity "Learn more about substrate specificity from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and binding, and enantio, regio, and [stereo selectivities](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/stereoselectivity "Learn more about stereo selectivities from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). Additionally, enzyme engineering, can be used to simultaneously improve the kinetics and stability of the enzyme (catalytic enzyme characteristics) and the yield of the enzyme and ease of [downstream processing](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/downstream-processing "Learn more about downstream processing from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (enzyme productivity). Furthermore, the most recent advancement in enzyme technology is enzyme engineering, which uses different genetic engineering techniques to target several biological molecules' properties at once [[[21]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024087048#bib21), [[22]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024087048#bib22), [[23]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024087048#bib23)]. Unfortunately, determining which mutations are essential when developing a novel enzyme is difficult [[24](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024087048#bib24)], which extends the time needed to engineer proteins [[[25]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024087048#bib25), [[26]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024087048#bib26), [[27]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024087048#bib27), [[28]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024087048#bib28)]. By developing a variety of new and enhanced biocatalysts suitable for a wide range of biocatalysis-based processes in industries, recent developments in genetic engineering techniques, in addition to the introduction of numerous highly efficient computational tools, have revolutionized the field of protein and enzyme engineering. Most biocatalysis-based sectors, including the [pharmaceutical industry](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/pharmaceutical-industry "Learn more about pharmaceutical industry from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), will likely rely on highly efficient and specifically designed enzymes in the near future to produce [bulk](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/immunology-and-microbiology/volume "Learn more about bulk from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and fine chemicals [[22](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024087048#bib22),[28](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024087048#bib28),[29](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024087048#bib29)]. [Lactobacillus](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/lactobacillus "Learn more about Lactobacillus from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")[kefir](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/kefir "Learn more about kefir from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")[ketoreductases](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/ketoreductase "Learn more about ketoreductases from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (KRED) are employed to catalyze the conversion of a [ketone](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/ketone "Learn more about ketone from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") intermediate into a chiral alcohol by reduction. Ketoreductase-mediated processes are employed in the production of intermediates for diverse pharmaceuticals, including montelukast, [atorvastatin](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/atorvastatin "Learn more about atorvastatin from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), [duloxetine](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/duloxetine "Learn more about duloxetine from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), [ezetimibe](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/ezetimibe "Learn more about ezetimibe from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), [crizotinib](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/crizotinib "Learn more about crizotinib from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and [phenylephrine](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/phenylephrine "Learn more about phenylephrine from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") [[30](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024087048#bib30),[31](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024087048#bib31)]. A highly promising instance of protein engineering involves the synthesis of [antidiabetic](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/antidiabetic-agent "Learn more about antidiabetic from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")[sitagliptin](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/sitagliptin "Learn more about sitagliptin from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") by Codexis and Merck [[11](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024087048#bib11),[27](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024087048#bib27),[28](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024087048#bib28)]. The process involved utilizing a specifically engineered enzyme called R-selective [transaminase](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/transaminase "Learn more about transaminase from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), which was produced through directed evolution. This enzyme was employed to carry out the asymmetric [amination](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/amination "Learn more about amination from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") of prositagliplitin ketone, resulting in the production of [sitagliptin](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/sitagliptin "Learn more about sitagliptin from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") with an [enantiopurity](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/enantiopurity "Learn more about enantiopurity from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") of 99.95% [[28](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024087048#bib28)]. [Diagnostic enzyme](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/diagnostic-enzyme "Learn more about Diagnostic enzyme from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") development is receiving attention in pharmaceutical and medical research. Abstract
--------

Click to copy section link Section link copied!

![Image 9: Abstract Image](https://pubs.acs.org/cms/10.1021/acs.jafc.5c03673/asset/images/medium/jf5c03673_0004.gif)

Enzyme preparations are highly valued in various industries due to their specificity and eco-friendliness. However, natural enzymes often face challenges in extreme pH conditions due to their narrow optimal pH ranges. This limitation highlights the need for enzyme modification that can expand their pH adaptability, enhance stability and activity across varying pH levels, and ultimately increase their industrial utility. To address these challenges, recent advancements in protein engineering have equipped scientists with powerful tools to tailor enzymatic properties. Techniques such as rational design, directed evolution, and computational biology now allow for the precise manipulation of enzyme structures and their functional dynamics. These strategies aim to bridge the inherent limitations of natural enzymes with the stringent requirements of industrial processes, especially in environments in which pH levels can fluctuate significantly. This article focuses on the field of protein engineering and provides a comprehensive review of the latest advancements in modification strategies for acid-tolerant and alkali-tolerant enzymes. It covers a variety of aspects including sequence-guided bioinformatics analysis, structural modifications, regulation of the endogenous force microenvironment, and surface engineering. This study aims to offer diverse perspectives and technical support to help enzymes adapt to the complex and ever-changing landscape of industrial applications.

# Automated in vivo enzyme engineering accelerates biocatalyst optimization

[*Nature Communications*](/ncomms)
**volume 15**, Article number: 3447 (2024)
[Cite this article](#citeas)

24k Accesses

43 Citations

55 Altmetric

[Metrics details](/articles/s41467-024-46574-4/metrics)

### Subjects

## Abstract

Achieving cost-competitive bio-based processes requires development of stable and selective biocatalysts. Their realization through in vitro enzyme characterization and engineering is mostly low throughput and labor-intensive. Therefore, strategies for increasing throughput while diminishing manual labor are gaining momentum, such as in vivo screening and evolution campaigns. Computational tools like machine learning further support enzyme engineering efforts by widening the explorable design space. Here, we propose an integrated solution to enzyme engineering challenges whereby ML-guided, automated workflows (including library generation, implementation of hypermutation systems, adapted laboratory evolution, and in vivo growth-coupled selection) could be realized to accelerate pipelines towards superior biocatalysts.

## Introduction

The development of tailored and efficient bio-based processes is essential for applications as diverse as biopharmaceutical production, industrial biotechnology, food technology, crop improvement, and bioremediation. To establish such profitable bio-based processes, biocatalysts that can perform substrate-to-product conversions with high volumetric productivities (gproduct L−1 h−1), yields (gproduct gsubstrate−1), and selectivities (enantiomeric excess) are essential[1](/articles/s41467-024-46574-4#ref-CR1 "Nielsen, J. & Keasling, J. D. Engineering cellular metabolism. Cell 164, 1185–1197 (2016)."). To reach improvements in these performance indicators and optimize chemical conversions, enzyme engineering has been developed as one of the pillars of synthetic biology[2](/articles/s41467-024-46574-4#ref-CR2 "Erb, T. J., Jones, P. R. & Bar-Even, A. Synthetic metabolism: metabolic engineering meets enzyme design. Curr. Opin. Chem. Biol. 37, 56–62 (2017). The idea of synthetic metabolism is defined as a level of metabolic engineering with an increased design and solution space beyond what nature has evolved for."), realizing enzyme optimization and development from the single reaction step to entire metabolic pathways[2](/articles/s41467-024-46574-4#ref-CR2 "Erb, T. J., Jones, P. R. & Bar-Even, A. Synthetic metabolism: metabolic engineering meets enzyme design. Curr. Opin. Chem. Biol. 37, 56–62 (2017). The idea of synthetic metabolism is defined as a level of metabolic engineering with an increased design and solution space beyond what nature has evolved for."),[3](/articles/s41467-024-46574-4#ref-CR3 "Volk, M. J. et al. Metabolic engineering: methodologies and applications. Chem. Rev. 9, 5521–5570 (2022).").

Current efforts in bioengineering aim at designing biological systems that provide enzymatic activities beyond what has been developed and optimized by nature[2](/articles/s41467-024-46574-4#ref-CR2 "Erb, T. J., Jones, P. R. & Bar-Even, A. Synthetic metabolism: metabolic engineering meets enzyme design. Curr. Opin. Chem. Biol. 37, 56–62 (2017). The idea of synthetic metabolism is defined as a level of metabolic engineering with an increased design and solution space beyond what nature has evolved for."),[4](/articles/s41467-024-46574-4#ref-CR4 "Chen, K. & Arnold, F. H. Engineering new catalytic activities in enzymes. Nat. Catal. 3, 203–213 (2020)."),[5](/articles/s41467-024-46574-4#ref-CR5 "Tan, D., Xue, Y. S., Aibaidula, G. & Chen, G. Q. Unsterile and continuous production of polyhydroxybutyrate by Halomonas TD01. Bioresour. Technol. 102, 8130–TD8136 (2011)."). Implementing these innovations can further develop a bio-based economy[6](#ref-CR6 "Clomburg, J. M., Crumbley, A. M. & Gonzalez, R. Industrial biomanufacturing: the future of chemical production. Science 355, aag0804 (2017)."),[7](#ref-CR7 "Zhang, Y. H. P., Sun, J. & Ma, Y. Biomanufacturing: history and perspective. J. Ind. Microbiol. Biotechnol. 44, 773–784 (2017)."),[8](/articles/s41467-024-46574-4#ref-CR8 "Buller, R. et al. From nature to industry: harnessing enzymes for biocatalysis. Science 382, eadh8615 (2023)."). Hence, it is desirable to design novel, new-to-nature enzymatic activities as the key parts needed to assemble complete synthetic pathways[2](/articles/s41467-024-46574-4#ref-CR2 "Erb, T. J., Jones, P. R. & Bar-Even, A. Synthetic metabolism: metabolic engineering meets enzyme design. Curr. Opin. Chem. Biol. 37, 56–62 (2017). The idea of synthetic metabolism is defined as a level of metabolic engineering with an increased design and solution space beyond what nature has evolved for."),[9](/articles/s41467-024-46574-4#ref-CR9 "Arnold, F. H. Directed evolution: bringing new chemistry to life. Angew. Chem. Int. Ed. Engl. 57, 4143–4148 (2018).") or used in enzyme-driven catalysis applications directly in synthetic processes (e.g., in the striking case of in vitro conversion of CO2/H2 or methanol into starch[10](/articles/s41467-024-46574-4#ref-CR10 "Cai, T. et al. Cell-free chemoenzymatic starch synthesis from carbon dioxide. Science 373, 1523–1527 (2021).")). However, creating and optimizing such new-to-nature reactions is a challenging task, for which the use of rational protein design accompanied by in vitro enzyme activity measurements or adaptive laboratory evolution (ALE) might not be sufficient[11](/articles/s41467-024-46574-4#ref-CR11 "Molina, R. S. et al. In vivo hypermutation and continuous evolution. Nat. Rev. Methods Primers 2, 37 (2022). Extensive review on the use of in vivo hypermutator techniques for continuous evolution."),[12](/articles/s41467-024-46574-4#ref-CR12 "Wu, Y., Jameel, A., Xing, X. H. & Zhang, C. Advanced strategies and tools to facilitate and streamline microbial adaptive laboratory evolution. Trends Biotechnol. 40, 38–59 (2022).").

At this point, directed evolution comes in handy, as it allows to perform Darwinian evolution in a test tube by increasing mutation and recombination rates within a target gene[13](/articles/s41467-024-46574-4#ref-CR13 "Wang, Y. et al. Directed evolution: methodologies and applications. Chem. Rev. 121, 12384–12444 (2021)."),[14](/articles/s41467-024-46574-4#ref-CR14 "Packer, M. S. & Liu, D. R. Methods for the directed evolution of proteins. Nat. Rev. Genet. 16, 379–394 (2015)."). Two types of directed evolution approaches are possible and differ in the environment where the evolution takes place. In vitro-directed evolution occurs outside a living organism, whereas in vivo evolution takes place within living systems. Both strategies have pros- and cons- which have been discussed elsewhere[15](/articles/s41467-024-46574-4#ref-CR15 "Golynskiy, M. V., Haugner, J. C. 3rd, Morelli, A., Morrone, D. & Seelig, B. In vitro evolution of enzymes. Methods Mol. Biol. 978, 73–92 (2013)."),[16](/articles/s41467-024-46574-4#ref-CR16 "Badran, A. H. & Liu, D. R. In vivo continuous directed evolution. Curr. Opin. Chem. Biol. 24, 1–10 (2015)."). In recent years, in vivo-directed evolution approaches have emerged as promising tools to use in protein engineering campaigns[11](/articles/s41467-024-46574-4#ref-CR11 "Molina, R. S. et al. In vivo hypermutation and continuous evolution. Nat. Rev. Methods Primers 2, 37 (2022). Extensive review on the use of in vivo hypermutator techniques for continuous evolution."),[16](/articles/s41467-024-46574-4#ref-CR16 "Badran, A. H. & Liu, D. R. In vivo continuous directed evolution. Curr. Opin. Chem. Biol. 24, 1–10 (2015)."). The use of these approaches combined with growth-coupled selection (meaning coupling the enzymatic activity of interest to microbial fitness) has been applied for different optimization strategies[17](/articles/s41467-024-46574-4#ref-CR17 "Li, Z., Deng, Y. & Yang, G. Y. Growth-coupled high throughput selection for directed enzyme evolution. Biotechnol. Adv. 68, 108238 (2023)."),[18](/articles/s41467-024-46574-4#ref-CR18 "Chen, J., Wang, Y., Zheng, P. & Sun, J. Engineering synthetic auxotrophs for growth-coupled directed protein evolution. Trends Biotechnol. 40, 773–776 (2022)."). At the same time, automated biofoundries are becoming pivotal in supporting high-throughput efforts for engineering biology[19](#ref-CR19 "Gurdo, N., Volke, D. C., McCloskey, D. & Nikel, P. I. Automating the design-build-test-learn cycle towards next-generation bacterial cell factories. N. Biotechnol. 74, 1–15 (2023). This review discusses in detail recent advances in automating the design-build-test-learn pipeline."),[20](#ref-CR20 "Tellechea-Luzardo, J., Otero-Muras, I., Goni-Moreno, A. & Carbonell, P. Engineering biological systems using automated biofoundries. Metab. Eng. 42, 98–108 (2017)."),[22](/articles/s41467-024-46574-4#ref-CR22 "Hillson, N. et al. Building a global alliance of biofoundries. Nat. Commun. 10, 2040 (2019)."). Hence, the use of these infrastructures for protein engineering is gaining momentum[23](/articles/s41467-024-46574-4#ref-CR23 "Yu, T., Boob, A. G., Singh, N., Su, Y. & Zhao, H. In vitro continuous protein evolution empowered by machine learning and automation. Cell Syst. 14, 633–644 (2023). Review on advancements in machine learning and lab automation for rapid protein engineering through directed evolution."). Moreover, the use of artificial intelligence (AI) and machine learning (ML) is aiding important endeavors in the design of new biological systems, from protein- to organism level[24](#ref-CR24 "Lawson, C. E. et al. Machine learning for metabolic engineering: a review. Metab. Eng. 63, 34–60 (2021)."),[25](#ref-CR25 "Kim, G. B., Kim, W. J., Kim, H. U. & Lee, S. Y. Machine learning applications in systems metabolic engineering. Curr. Opin. Biotechnol. 64, 1–9 (2020)."),[26](#ref-CR26 "Cheng, Y. et al. Machine learning for metabolic pathway optimization: a review. Comput. Struct. Biotechnol. J. 21, 2381–2393 (2023)."),[27](/articles/s41467-024-46574-4#ref-CR27 "Yang, K. K., Wu, Z. & Arnold, F. H. Machine-learning-guided directed evolution for protein engineering. Nat. Methods 16, 687–694 (2019)."). Therefore, we are witnessing a paradigm shift in our ability and capacity to engineer biological systems. A combination of these technologies might result in the establishment of self-driving labs and workflows, which potentially accelerate scientific discoveries and innovation while reducing human errors[28](#ref-CR28 "Rapp, J. T., Bremer, B. J. & Romero, P. A. Self-driving laboratories to autonomously navigate the protein fitness landscape. Nat. Chem. Eng. 1, 97–107 (2024). In this article, we discuss how the integration of ML, in vivo continuous evolution, and the use of automated biofoundries will accelerate the generation of new and competitive biocatalysts capable of supporting the transition towards a circular bio-based economy. Definitions of the most important technical terms used within the text are described in the Box [1](/articles/s41467-024-46574-4#Sec13).

### An integrated workflow for accelerating in vivo enzyme engineering

To enable the workflow proposed in this article, different fields of expertize need to be integrated (Fig. [1](/articles/s41467-024-46574-4#Fig1)). In brief, ML is used as input for (i) predicting the modifications required for engineering the target enzyme(s) and (ii) supporting the design of auxotroph selection strains by suggesting target genes to delete. Then, these selection strains are created through gene deletions. Subsequently, in vivo hypermutators can be exploited to increase the mutation rate within the target gene. Following the principle of growth-coupled selection, high-throughput and continuous cultivation platforms can be used for enriching the microbial population with clones containing the evolved target enzyme(s). Finally, sequencing of the enriched clones can inform the success of the evolution campaign. This step can also benefit from the use of ML-guided computational tools. If necessary, this pipeline can be iterated through several rounds.

![figure 1](//media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs41467-024-46574-4/MediaObjects/41467_2024_46574_Fig1_HTML.png)

This workflow aims at generating superior catalysts by combining the use of machine learning, growth-coupled selection, in vivo hypermutators, and high-throughput cultivations. Each part can be envisioned as a stand-alone module, which can, in principle, be connected using mobile robot units. Created with BioRender.com.

The abovementioned steps should be intended as stand-alone workflows, which can be integrated by mobile robot units (Fig. [1](/articles/s41467-024-46574-4#Fig1)). In the following sections of the manuscript, we dive more in-depth into the different aspects of this pipeline, eventually suggesting their integration using state-of-the-art automated workflows. Finally, we discuss caveats and limitations of this concept.

### Enzyme engineering generates improved biocatalysts

Mastering enzyme engineering is vital to enable the optimization of existing bioprocesses or the exploration of new ones. Here, we cannot give a comprehensive overview of this large and rapidly developing area of research, but rather present key approaches and concepts. For a more detailed summary, the reader is referred to the existing literature[4](/articles/s41467-024-46574-4#ref-CR4 "Chen, K. & Arnold, F. H. Engineering new catalytic activities in enzymes. Nat. Catal. 3, 203–213 (2020)."),[31](#ref-CR31 "Lovelock, S. L. et al. The road to fully programmable protein catalysis. Nature 606, 49–58 (2022)."),[32](#ref-CR32 "R., C & Maranas, C. D. From directed evolution to computational enzyme engineering—a review. AiChE J. 66 
                  https://par.nsf.gov/servlets/purl/10170897
                  
                 (2020)."),[33](/articles/s41467-024-46574-4#ref-CR33 "Qu, G., Li, A., Acevedo-Rocha, C. G., Sun, Z. & Reetz, M. T. The crucial role of methodology development in directed evolution of selective enzymes. Angew. Chem. Int. Ed. Engl. 59, 13204–13231 (2020).").

The targets for enzyme engineering are highly diverse. To broaden the substrate range, the active site must be opened and remodeled; to improve the substrate specificity or enantioselectivity, the active site must be altered to only accommodate one type of desired substrate or intermediate[33](/articles/s41467-024-46574-4#ref-CR33 "Qu, G., Li, A., Acevedo-Rocha, C. G., Sun, Z. & Reetz, M. T. The crucial role of methodology development in directed evolution of selective enzymes. Angew. Chem. Int. Ed. Engl. 59, 13204–13231 (2020)."); to develop novel catalytic functions, general principles of catalysis and transition state stabilization have to be applied to modify a suitable scaffold enzyme. To increase the thermal stability of enzymes and allow the catalysis of industrial processes at high temperatures, enzymes must be modified by introducing additional hydrogen bonds and salt bridges, rigidizing flexible residues, creating a more compact core region, or decreasing surface area hydrophobicity[34](/articles/s41467-024-46574-4#ref-CR34 "Nezhad, N. G. et al. Thermostability engineering of industrial enzymes through structure modification. Appl. Microbiol. Biotechnol. 106, 4845–4866 (2022)."),[35](/articles/s41467-024-46574-4#ref-CR35 "Sun, Z., Liu, Q., Qu, G., Feng, Y. & Reetz, M. T. Utility of B-factors in protein science: interpreting rigidity, flexibility, and internal motion and engineering thermostability. Chem. Rev. 119, 1626–1665 (2019)."). Computational tools and predictions can help in identifying relevant amino acid residues and regions of interest to be mutated[36](/articles/s41467-024-46574-4#ref-CR36 "Planas-Iglesias, J. et al. Computational design of enzymes for biotechnological applications. Biotechnol. Adv. 47, 107696 (2021)."),[37](/articles/s41467-024-46574-4#ref-CR37 "Sumbalova, L., Stourac, J., Martinek, T., Bednar, D. & Damborsky, J. HotSpot Wizard 3.0: web server for automated design of mutations and smart libraries based on sequence input information. Nucleic Acids Res. 46, W356–W362 (2018)."). Prediction of relevant residues to be mutated can be supported by experimentally determined protein structures, or—in their absence—by the data-driven protein structure prediction tool AlphaFold[38](/articles/s41467-024-46574-4#ref-CR38 "Jumper, J. et al. Highly accurate protein structure prediction with AlphaFold. Nature 596, 583–589 (2021)."), which has made high-quality protein structure models easily available for the global research community since 2021.

Previously established techniques of enzyme engineering, such as rational mutagenesis based on in silico predictions or structure analysis, semi-rational mutagenesis (i.e., the combination of site-directed mutagenesis with random mutagenesis or directed evolution[39](/articles/s41467-024-46574-4#ref-CR39 "Chica, R. A., Doucet, N. & Pelletier, J. N. Semi-rational approaches to engineering enzyme activity: combining the benefits of directed evolution and rational design. Curr. Opin. Biotechnol. 16, 378–384 (2005).")), and directed evolution of a suitable parent enzyme[13](/articles/s41467-024-46574-4#ref-CR13 "Wang, Y. et al. Directed evolution: methodologies and applications. Chem. Rev. 121, 12384–12444 (2021)."),[40](/articles/s41467-024-46574-4#ref-CR40 "McLure, R. J., Radford, S. E. & Brockwell, D. J. High-throughput directed evolution: a golden era for protein science. Trends Chem. 4, 278–291 (2022)."), can provide the starting points for screenings of improved biocatalysts and iterative cycles of enzyme development (Fig. [2](/articles/s41467-024-46574-4#Fig2)). However, efficiently enhancing catalytic properties of enzymes requires maneuvering through complex and rugged fitness landscapes, where the relationship between enzyme sequence (genotype) and functional characteristics (phenotype) is difficult to predict. This means that optimization trajectories frequently result in diminishing returns (i.e., additional mutations only result in minimal improvements of an enzyme) and undesired tradeoff effects (e.g., substrate specificity is improved, but enzyme turnover number is strongly decreased)[41](/articles/s41467-024-46574-4#ref-CR41 "Tokuriki, N. et al. Diminishing returns and tradeoffs constrain the laboratory optimization of an enzyme. Nat. Commun. 3, 1257 (2012)."),[42](/articles/s41467-024-46574-4#ref-CR42 "Tawfik, D. S. Accuracy-rate tradeoffs: how do enzymes meet demands of selectivity and catalytic efficiency. Curr. Opin. Chem. Biol. 21, 73–80 (2014)."). When the abovementioned methods no longer yield enhancements, it often remains uncertain if an enzyme has already reached its maximum catalytic efficiency, or if there are other possible combinations of mutations that could generate further improvements[43](/articles/s41467-024-46574-4#ref-CR43 "Goldsmith, M. & Tawfik, D. S. Enzyme engineering: reaching the maximal catalytic efficiency peak. Curr. Opin. Struct. Biol. 47, 140–150 (2017)."). Therefore, the construction of large combinatorial enzyme libraries is a key approach in enzyme engineering (Fig. [2](/articles/s41467-024-46574-4#Fig2)). By introducing diversity into enzyme sequences, libraries can be screened or selected for desired properties. High-throughput screening methods, including droplet-based microfluidics and fluorescence-activated cell sorting (FACS), enable the isolation and identification of enzymes with improved features, as long as a suitable readout is available (Fig. [3](/articles/s41467-024-46574-4#Fig3)).

![figure 2](//media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs41467-024-46574-4/MediaObjects/41467_2024_46574_Fig2_HTML.png)

This includes increasing enzyme activity, substrate specificity, or enantioselectivity, introducing novel reactivities, or improving protein stability, among other goals. Methods that are applied for this purpose range from rational mutagenesis of key amino acids to semi-rational approaches and directed evolution of gene sequences from DNA libraries. De novo enzyme design can be applied to generate biocatalysts that are free from the constraints of existing enzymes. Created with BioRender.com.

![figure 3](//media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs41467-024-46574-4/MediaObjects/41467_2024_46574_Fig3_HTML.png)

The figure shows and scores the properties of the two methods for several key performance indicators (KPIs). Moreover, the use of additional technical improvements to the approaches is included, such as random mutagenesis (RM), directed evolution (DE), hypermutators and adaptive laboratory evolution (ALE). The impact of these improvements on the KPIs is also depicted in the figure.

In addition to the previously discussed methods, ML approaches are now being more commonly employed to identify data patterns that aid in forecasting protein structures, enhancing enzyme stability, solubility, and function, predicting substrate specificity, and facilitating rational de novo protein design[27](/articles/s41467-024-46574-4#ref-CR27 "Yang, K. K., Wu, Z. & Arnold, F. H. Machine-learning-guided directed evolution for protein engineering. Nat. Methods 16, 687–694 (2019)."),[44](/articles/s41467-024-46574-4#ref-CR44 "Mazurenko, S., Prokop, Z. & Damoborsky, J. Machine learning in enzyme engineering. ACS Catal. 10, 1210–1223 (2020)."),[45](/articles/s41467-024-46574-4#ref-CR45 "Kouba, P. et al. Machine learning-guided protein engineering. ACS Catal. 13, 13863–13895 (2023).").

### De novo enzyme design complements enzyme engineering

The aim of de novo enzyme design is the development of novel enzymes with desired functions from scratch, without relying on naturally occurring enzymes as starting points. Various protein design algorithms, such as Rosetta[46](/articles/s41467-024-46574-4#ref-CR46 "Alford, R. F. et al. The rosetta all-atom energy function for macromolecular modeling and design. J. Chem. Theory Comput 13, 3031–3048 (2017)."),[47](/articles/s41467-024-46574-4#ref-CR47 "Kaufmann, K. W., Lemmon, G. H., Deluca, S. L., Sheehan, J. H. & Meiler, J. Practically useful: what the Rosetta protein modeling suite can do for you. Biochemistry 49, 2987–2998 (2010).") (<https://www.rosettacommons.org/>), have been developed to predict and optimize enzyme sequences based on desired functions. Rosetta relies on the mechanistic modelling of proteins using energy fields to guide the design process and explore the vast sequence space for enzyme engineering[31](/articles/s41467-024-46574-4#ref-CR31 "Lovelock, S. L. et al. The road to fully programmable protein catalysis. Nature 606, 49–58 (2022)."). Already 15 years ago, the first high-profile designer enzymes, e.g., for the catalysis of retro-aldol reactions, were reported[48](/articles/s41467-024-46574-4#ref-CR48 "Jiang, L. et al. De novo computational design of retro-aldol enzymes. Science 319, 1387–1391 (2008)."). Some current highlights in this rapidly growing field include the de novo design of an eight stranded β-barrel protein that functions as a retro-aldolase, whose activity and stereoselectivity were further improved using directed evolution[49](/articles/s41467-024-46574-4#ref-CR49 "Kipnis, Y. et al. Design and optimization of enzymatic activity in a de novo beta-barrel scaffold. Protein Sci. 31, e4405 (2022)."), and the creation of artificial luciferase enzymes from scratch, whose catalytic efficiency is comparable to that of natural luciferases, while having a much higher substrate specificity and very high thermostability[50](/articles/s41467-024-46574-4#ref-CR50 "Yeh, A. H. et al. De novo design of luciferases using deep learning. Nature 614, 774–780 (2023)."). The de novo design of enzymes that bind complex cofactors, such as heme irons, is also not an obstacle anymore: recently, the creation of a heme enzyme with a tunable substrate-binding pocket and its further engineering into an efficient carbene transferase was reported[51](/articles/s41467-024-46574-4#ref-CR51 "Kalvet, I. et al. Design of heme enzymes with a tunable substrate binding pocket adjacent to an open metal coordination site. J. Am. Chem. Soc. 145, 14307–14315 (2023)."). The combination of protein design with iterative mutagenesis for efficient enzyme engineering should not be underestimated. A good example is the conversion of a designed enzyme with modest activity for carbon-carbon bond formation between aldehydes and enones[52](/articles/s41467-024-46574-4#ref-CR52 "Bjelic, S. et al. Computational design of enone-binding proteins with catalytic activity for the Morita-Baylis-Hillman reaction. ACS Chem. Biol. 8, 749–757 (2013).") into a highly efficient biocatalyst via fourteen rounds of both local and global mutagenesis, coupled to high-throughput spectrophotometric assays as time-efficient readout[53](/articles/s41467-024-46574-4#ref-CR53 "Crawshaw, R. et al. Engineering an efficient and enantioselective enzyme for the Morita-Baylis-Hillman reaction. Nat. Chem. 14, 313–320 (2022).").

It seems likely that de novo protein design will be feasible and widely used for all types of enzymes in the near future. The fine-tuning of the deep learning neural network RoseTTAFold[54](/articles/s41467-024-46574-4#ref-CR54 "Baek, M. et al. Accurate prediction of protein structures and interactions using a three-track neural network. Science 373, 871–876 (2021).") on protein structure denoising tasks resulted in RFdiffusion, a generative model of protein backbones with outstanding performance on protein monomer design, enzyme active site scaffolding (Fig. [2](/articles/s41467-024-46574-4#Fig2)), and metal-binding protein design, which only requires simple molecular specifications as input[55](/articles/s41467-024-46574-4#ref-CR55 "Watson, J. L. et al. De novo design of protein structure and function with RFdiffusion. Nature 620, 1089–1100 (2023).").

Computational enzyme design is especially valuable to realize a novel metabolic pathway in which a natural enzyme for one reaction step is lacking[56](/articles/s41467-024-46574-4#ref-CR56 "Siegel, J. B. et al. Computational protein design enables a novel one-carbon assimilation pathway. Proc. Natl. Acad. Sci. 112, 3704–3709 (2015)."). Here, de novo design can supply enzyme candidates that catalyze the desired conversion, often only with initially low activities. These enzymes can subsequently be improved by mutagenesis and directed evolution, making it possible to implement efficient new-to-nature bioconversion routes.

### ML-supported pathway design increases the engineering design space

The design and implementation of non-natural metabolic pathways is a complex and highly time-consuming task. ML can alleviate this challenge by automating several stages of the pathway design process[24](/articles/s41467-024-46574-4#ref-CR24 "Lawson, C. E. et al. Machine learning for metabolic engineering: a review. Metab. Eng. 63, 34–60 (2021)."). Specifically, ML algorithms can efficiently predict and analyze metabolic reactions, aiding in retrobiosynthesis approaches (i.e., the identification of potential pathways for the production of specific, desired compounds[57](#ref-CR57 "Koch, M., Duigou, T. & Faulon, J.-L. Reinforcement learning for bioretrosynthesis. ACS Synth. Biol. 9, 157–168 (2020)."),[58](#ref-CR58 "Yu, T. et al. Machine learning-enabled retrobiosynthesis of molecules. Nat. Catal. 6, 137–151 (2023)."),[59](/articles/s41467-024-46574-4#ref-CR59 "Zheng, S. et al.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Utilizing machine learning and automation enhances enzyme design processes, leading to rapid optimization and improved performance in various industrial applications.
Addressing limitations such as enzyme stability and substrate specificity is crucial for enhancing the efficiency and applicability of engineered enzymes in diverse environments.
Through targeted modifications and novel design strategies, engineered enzymes can achieve higher catalytic efficiencies and broader substrate ranges, unlocking new industrial applications.

Engineered enzymes facilitate innovative biocatalytic processes, transforming production methods in sectors like pharmaceuticals, biofuels, and food processing, thereby enhancing overall productivity.
The development of green biocatalysts contributes to sustainable practices by reducing environmental impact, minimizing waste, and promoting the use of renewable resources in industrial processes.
